article.title: Meta Commits to Over 1 Gigawatt of Custom AI Chips With Broadcom in Multi-Year Deal That Extends Through 2029
article.summary: Meta and Broadcom expand their partnership to co-develop multiple generations of MTIA chips, marking the first custom AI silicon built on a 2nm process.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Meta Platforms and Broadcom announced on April 14 an expanded partnership under which Meta will deploy more than one gigawatt of its custom-designed Meta Training and Inference Accelerator chips, with plans to scale to multiple gigawatts through 2029. The deal covers chip design, advanced packaging, and networking, and will produce what Broadcom describes as the first custom AI silicon manufactured on a 2-nanometer process node, according to [Broadcom's press release](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/14/3273998/19933/en/Broadcom-Announces-Extended-Partnership-with-Meta-to-Deploy-Technology-to-Support-Multi-Gigawatts-of-Meta-s-Custom-Silicon-MTIA.html).

The agreement deepens Meta's push to build its own AI hardware rather than rely solely on merchant silicon from Nvidia and AMD. It follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/19-meta-signs-multibillion-dollar-deal-to-lease-google-tpus-as-big-tech-races-to-diversify-beyond-nvidia) on Meta signing a multibillion-dollar deal to lease Google TPUs, part of a broader pattern of hyperscalers diversifying their chip procurement strategies.

## What We Know

Meta has committed to an initial deployment exceeding one gigawatt of MTIA compute capacity, with Broadcom CEO Hock Tan calling it "just the beginning of a sustained, multi-generation roadmap," according to [Broadcom's announcement](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/14/3273998/19933/en/Broadcom-Announces-Extended-Partnership-with-Meta-to-Deploy-Technology-to-Support-Multi-Gigawatts-of-Meta-s-Custom-Silicon-MTIA.html). The partnership extends through 2029 and covers four new MTIA chip generations that Meta is developing within a two-year window, as [reported by SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/14/meta-doubles-partnership-broadcom-committing-1-gigawatt-custom-ai-processors/).

The MTIA chips are application-specific integrated circuits built on Broadcom's XPU platform, purpose-built to optimize inference and low-precision processing workloads including ranking, recommendations, and generative AI, according to [Meta's official announcement](https://about.fb.com/news/2026/04/meta-partners-with-broadcom-to-co-develop-custom-ai-silicon/). The design tightly couples logic, memory, and high-speed I/O, and Broadcom will also supply high-radix Ethernet switches, optical connectivity products, and rack-scale interconnects to link them across Meta's expanding AI compute clusters, per the [Broadcom press release](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/04/14/3273998/19933/en/Broadcom-Announces-Extended-Partnership-with-Meta-to-Deploy-Technology-to-Support-Multi-Gigawatts-of-Meta-s-Custom-Silicon-MTIA.html).

Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg said the partnership "will give us greater performance and efficiency for everything we're building," according to [Meta's blog post](https://about.fb.com/news/2026/04/meta-partners-with-broadcom-to-co-develop-custom-ai-silicon/). The original MTIA chip debuted in 2023, and Meta has already begun using it to run recommendation systems, as [SiliconANGLE noted](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/14/meta-doubles-partnership-broadcom-committing-1-gigawatt-custom-ai-processors/).

As part of the expanded relationship, Broadcom CEO Hock Tan will step down from Meta's board of directors and transition to an advisory role focused on guiding Meta's custom silicon roadmap and infrastructure investments, according to [Meta](https://about.fb.com/news/2026/04/meta-partners-with-broadcom-to-co-develop-custom-ai-silicon/).

## What We Don't Know

Neither company disclosed the financial terms of the deal. It remains unclear how the MTIA chips will be divided between training and inference workloads in practice, or which specific Meta products they will power beyond recommendations. The 2-nanometer claim also leaves open which foundry will manufacture the chips; TSMC is widely expected to be the sole supplier of 2nm capacity in the relevant timeframe, but neither Meta nor Broadcom confirmed this.

It is also uncertain how the new MTIA generations will coexist with Meta's existing Nvidia GPU fleet and the Google TPU capacity it recently secured. Meta's capital expenditure guidance for 2026 ranges from $115 billion to $135 billion, as [SiliconANGLE reported](https://siliconangle.com/2026/04/14/meta-doubles-partnership-broadcom-committing-1-gigawatt-custom-ai-processors/), but the portion allocated specifically to MTIA deployment has not been disclosed.

## Analysis

The deal is Broadcom's third major custom AI silicon agreement in three weeks, following similar arrangements with Google and Anthropic. The pattern underscores a structural shift in AI hardware procurement: hyperscalers are increasingly commissioning bespoke chips rather than buying off-the-shelf GPUs, seeking better performance-per-watt and total cost of ownership for the specific workloads that dominate their data centers.

For Meta, the move is a logical next step in a multi-pronged chip strategy. Leasing Google TPUs gave the company near-term diversification away from Nvidia; building custom silicon with Broadcom gives it longer-term control over its hardware roadmap. The 2nm process node, if realized on schedule, would place MTIA among the most advanced AI accelerators in production, potentially narrowing the performance gap with Nvidia's next-generation Blackwell and Rubin architectures.

The shift also carries risk. Custom ASICs are inherently less flexible than general-purpose GPUs, and Meta's AI workloads are evolving rapidly as it pushes into generative AI alongside its established recommendation systems. If the workload mix shifts faster than the chip roadmap can adapt, the company could find itself locked into hardware that underperforms on emerging tasks.
